coredata.dc:title: Polyvinyl alcohol-derived-carbon quantum dots based fluorometric “On-Off” probe for moxifloxacin detection in milk and egg samples
coredata.dc:creator: Sajwan, Reena K.
Kumar Himanshu, Jayendra
Solanki, Pratima R.
coredata.dc:description: Unconditional use of antibiotics triggered the process of bacterial resistance and causes major health problems. Nowadays, antibiotics majorly used in animals not only for infection treatment but also as mass promotor. The excess amount of antibiotics residue in animal derived foods which accelerate antibiotic resistance (ABR). So, here, a simple and quick carbon quantum dots(CQDs) based fluorometric “On-Off” probe was developed for detection of moxifloxacin (MOXI) in milk and egg samples. The CQDs emits blue emission and are uniformly distributed with average particle size 5.9 ± 0.22 nm. With MOXI, fluorescence intensity of CQDs at 372 nm decreased due to inner filter effect (IFE) and a new peak appeared at 508 nm correspondence to MOXI. The probe shows linear response with MOXI concentration varies as 0.025 µM – 15.0 µM with lower detection limit (LOD) of 6.34 nM. The real sample applicability test proved that the sensors have excellent efficacy for food applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Antibiotics
Fluorescence
“on–off” sensor
Moxifloxacin
Food samples

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid otherkwds primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Interferents study for the selectivity of the sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work completes without technical and financial support provided by institute and Department of Science and Technology (DST). Authors are thankful to Advanced Instrumentation & Research Facility (AIRF) JNU, New Delhi, for providing a TEM and TRFS characterization facility. The author Reena K. Sajwan is also grateful to DST, India for providing financial support and motivation through Women Scientist- A (DST/WOS-A/ET-11/2020) fellowship. Solanki thanks to Indian Council of Medical Research, (PAC-SCNS-PS-ICMR-1293) New Delhi, India and Department Health Research, Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Government of India (Grant No: R.11013/46/2021-GIA/HR) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Particle size distribution of CQDs shown in the TEM image. (B) HR-TEM image shows the inter-planar distance of CQDs. (C) Represented the average particle size of CQDs. (D) FTIR spectra of CQDs and (E) surface potential of CQDs.

(A) UV absorption, emission, and excitation spectra of CQDs, inset shows the visual image of CQDs under the exposure of UV-lamp. (B) CQDs emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths vary from 280 nm to 380 nm with a difference of 20 nm.

(A) Spectral overlapping between absorption spectra of MOXI (a) excitation (b) and emission (c) spectra of CQDs. (B) Decay time analysis of CQDs in the absence and presence of MOXI. (C) Schematic representation of the quenching mechanism of CQDs in the presence of MOXI.

(A) fluorescence intensity response of CQDs in addition of various concentrations of MOXI (0.025 μM – 15.0 µM). Inset of (A) shows the quenching in the fluorescence intensity of CQDs at peak 372 nm along with MOXI's concentration. (B) Represented the ratio between intensity peaks (F508/F372). (C) Selectivity analysis of sensor in absences (purple color) and presence (mustard color) of MOXI. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison between previously reported sensors for the detection of MOXI.

row.entry: hydrochloric acid (0.01 M) + Ammonium Reineckate + 50 %(v/v) acetone
carbon paste electrode modified with molecularly imprinted polymer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Practical application of developed sensor with all milk and egg samples.

head.ce:title: Polyvinyl alcohol-derived-carbon quantum dots based fluorometric “On-Off” probe for moxifloxacin detection in milk and egg samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nano-bio Laboratory, Special Center for Nanoscience, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi 110067, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nano-bio Laboratory, Special Center for Nanoscience, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi 110067, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, School of Life Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi Central University, Motihari, Bihar 845401, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biotechnology, School of Life Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi Central University, Motihari, Bihar 845401, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Unconditional use of antibiotics triggered the process of bacterial resistance and causes major health problems. Nowadays, antibiotics majorly used in animals not only for infection treatment but also as mass promotor. The excess amount of antibiotics residue in animal derived foods which accelerate antibiotic resistance (ABR). So, here, a simple and quick carbon quantum dots(CQDs) based fluorometric “On-Off” probe was developed for detection of moxifloxacin (MOXI) in milk and egg samples. The CQDs emits blue emission and are uniformly distributed with average particle size 5.9 ± 0.22 nm. With MOXI, fluorescence intensity of CQDs at 372 nm decreased due to inner filter effect (IFE) and a new peak appeared at 508 nm correspondence to MOXI. The probe shows linear response with MOXI concentration varies as 0.025 µM – 15.0 µM with lower detection limit (LOD) of 6.34 nM. The real sample applicability test proved that the sensors have excellent efficacy for food applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are used as life-saving drugs as they can terminate bacterial growth, which causes various infections and develops a threat to human health (Zaman et al., 2017). Due to the rampant overuse of antibiotics in human and animal food production sectors can have significant consequences for individuals and society while developing antibiotic resistance (ABR) (Fymat, 2017; Zaman et al., 2017). ABR occurs when bacteria resist the antibiotics meant for treating bacterial infection. The ABR has severe consequences such as prolonged illness, increased mortality rates, and increased burden to the medical society (Pulingam et al., 2022). The major sources of ABR is the overuse and misuse of antibiotics, prescribe for viral infections, which they are not effective, and prescribing broad-spectrum antibiotics instead of narrow-spectrum ones, which can lead to developed ABR (Zaman et al., 2017). Another factor contributing to ABR is using antibiotics in livestock and animal-derived food industries (Ibrahim et al., 2020; Moudgil et al., 2018). Antibiotics are used in animal feed not only to treat bacterial infection but more often as a growth promoter to enhance the animal's mass, leading to the development of ABR bacteria in these animals (Ngangom et al., 2019). When humans consume contaminated food, the resistive bacteria can be transmitted to humans through the food chain. The World Health Organization has declared ABR as one of the most significant public health challenges of our time, and it estimates that by 2050, ABR could cause up to 10 million deaths per year globally (Farooqui et al., 2018).

Among all the antibiotics, fluoroquinolones are most widely used to treat broad-spectrum bacterial infection. Due to their deep penetration ability into different tissues and organs makes them effective against a wide range of bacterial pathogens (Majdinasab et al., 2019). Moxifloxacin (MOXI) lies in fluoroquinolone class of antibiotics developed in the era of 4th generation which have antibacterial properties for broad spectrum gram-positive and negative bacteria. MOXI is commonly prescribed for urinary and respiratory tract infections, skin and soft tissue inflections treatment in humans and animals. It also treats tuberculosis and some forms of pneumonia (Sajwan & Singh, 2022). MOXI works by inhibiting the bacterial enzymes responsible for DNA replication and cell division, ultimately leading to bacterial death. Due to its countable effective against both grams positive and negative, this antibiotic comes under the category of broad spectrum antibiotics (Sajwan et al., 2021). The overused of MOXI have several side effects, including nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, headache, dizziness, and insomnia. Excess MOXI residing in animal-derived food samples can cause serious health problems. So, it is very necessary to monitor or scan the food and trace the residue of MOXI for the safety of human health. The residue of antibiotics in food samples should not be beyond the maximum tolerance limit of antibiotics (1.0 µg kg—1) set by food safety and standards of India.

The residue of the antibiotics have been detected by various analytical techniques like high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Abdelaziz et al., 2012), gas chromatography- mass- spectrometry (GC–MS), liquid chromatography- mass- spectrometry (LC-MS) (Zhao et al., 2022), spectrophotometry, spectrofluorometry (Kamruzzaman et al., 2012), capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Abdel-Gawad & Altharawi, 2023), electrochemical methods (Jiang et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2015), atomic absorption spectrometry (Al-Ghannam, 2008), and potentiometric (Hefnawy et al., 2014). The analytical techniques have inherent drawbacks, such as expensive equipment and lengthy procedures. A sensitive, selective, practical, reliable, and quick detection method for MOXI is highly desired to ensure human health and safety. Fluorescence method gained researcher’s attention because of its high sensitivity, selectivity, simplicity and rapidity, and well-understood principles like static and dynamic quenching (Zhang et al., 2020), photo induced electron transfer (PET) (Li et al., 2018; Sajwan et al., 2021), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Li et al., 2022), and inner filter effect (IFE) (Wei et al., 2020). With the benefits of affordability high selectivity, quick detection, and in-situ applications, fluorescent “On-Off” sensors seem to be excellent alternatives for detecting antibiotics.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a carbon-based nanomaterials which are majorly used in recent years due to their exclusive optical and electronic properties, biocompatibility, and low toxicity (Mao et al., 2010). CQDs are typically less than 10 nm and consist of a core of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms surrounded by functional groups. The functional groups define the chemical and physical properties of the CQDs, making them suitable for a range of applications (Molaei, 2020). The exceptional optical properties of CQDs are due to the effect of quantum confinement, which results in the size dependent fluorescence. CQDs having prominent fluorescence in visible and near infrared regions providing them additional advantages in the field of sensing, photo catalytic degradation, and bio-imaging applications (Vibhute et al., 2022). Apart from the optical and electronic properties CQDs also have low toxicity in cell and make them biocompatible and can be used as a promising agent in biomedical applications. Due the inherent fluorescent properties of CQDs it has been used as a sensing probe for the detection of various analyte such as, metal ions, toxin, pesticides, and antibiotics etc. In this directions, Li et al., have synthesized CQDs hydrothermally by taking starch and acetic acid as source materials and used then for the detection of MOXI. The reported method detected MOXI with linear range 3.3 × 10−7 to 2 × 10−6 M with LOD 2.59 nM (Li et al., 2022). Yanzhao et al., reported blue fluorescence CQDs synthesized using p-dihydroxybenzene and hydrazine hydrate throughhydrothermal and used for the detection of ampicillin with limit of detection 0.70 μM using treatment (Fu et al., 2020).

The synthesis of CQDs can be done by taking any carbon containing source materials, like chemicals, or plant based materials to make the synthesis easy and affordable (Das et al., 2018). In the past, many researchers used polymers like polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), polyvinyl pyrrole which act as capping and functionalizing agent used for the surface modification of CQDs (El-Shamy & Zayied, 2020). However, some researchers used polymer as a source precursor for the synthesis of CQDs (Ren et al., 2014). So far, PVA has been used as a capping agent for functionalizing nanomaterials to provide water solubility and make them suitable for biological/aqueous phased-based applications. Yang et al., have used cellulose fibers as carbon source for the synthesis of CQDs using hydrothermal method followed by Solvent casting method used to prepare the PVA/CQDs composites and used as a luminescent water-induced shape memory polymer (Yang et al., 2016). In this study, PVA is used as a source precursor for synthesis of CQDs.

In the present work, CQDs was synthesized using one-pot hydrothermal method using PVA as source material. The advantage of PVA is that, it has a backbone composed only of carbon atoms used as a source material and a capping agent in the synthesis of CQDs. The optical properties and surface properties of CQDs were characterized with spectroscopy techniques. CQDs were emitted the maximum blue emission when excited at 300 nm excited wavelength. When MOXI added to CQDs, CQD’s fluorescence intensity was quenched due to the IFE quenching mechanism. The fluorescence of CQDs linearly quenched with increasing concentration of MOXI varying from 0.025 µM to 15.0 µM. with lower limit of detection (LOD) 6.34 nM and lower limit of quantification (LOQ) 20.92 nM. The system can detect the lowest MOXI concentration in the test solution was 6.34 nM and the lowest MOXI in the test sample that the developed system can quantify was 20.92 nM. The selectivity and applicability of the sensor was tested in various interferents presented in the animal-derived food like milk and egg samples. The applicability results proven that the developed system has high sensitivity and selectivity to MOXI in food milk and egg samples and can be used as food monitoring application.

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) (C2H4O)n was brought from central drug house Pvt. Ltd. Moxifloxacin (MOXI) hydrochloride (C21H24FN3O4), bovine serum albumin (BSA), citric acid (CA) (C6H8O7), and l-cysteine (CYS) (C3H7NO2S) were carried out from Sigma- Aldrich. Kanamycin (KMC) (C18H36N4O11) gentamicin (GENTA) sulfates (C21H43N5O7. H2SO4), Ciprofloxacin (CIPRO) hydrochloride (C17H18FN3O4.HCl), ofloaxin (OFLO) (C18H20FN3O4), thiamphenicol (THIO) (C12H15Cl2NO5 S), florfenicol (FLOR) (C12 H14 Cl2FNO4S), norfloxacin (NOR) (C16H18FN3O3), levofloxacin (LEVO) (C18H20FN3O4), uric acid (URIC A) (C5H4N4O3), cholesterol (CHOL) (C27H46O), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (N2O6Zn·6H2O), sodium hydroxide pellets (NaOH), arginine (ARGI) (C6H14N4O2), urea (CH4N2O), casein (CASI) (C81 H125 N22 O39 P), methionine (METH) (C5H11NO2S) and ascorbic acid (AA) (C6H8O6), were obtained from SRL. Ltd. Folic acid (FA) (C19H19N2O6) and aspartic acid (ASPA A) (NH2CH (COOH) CH2.COOH) were obtained from CDH. Lead chloride (PbCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), and cadmium chloride hydrate (CdCl2·H2O) were purchased from Thomas Baker. Copper chloride (CuCl2), ferrous chloride (FeCl2·H2O), tetracycline (TETRA) hydrochloride (C22H24N2O6.HCl), and ampicillin (AMPHI) sodium salt (C16H18N3O4SNa) were brought from Hi-media Laboratory Pvt. Ltd. Calcium chloride (CaCl2) was purchased from Rankem Aventor India Limited. Glucose (GLU) (C6H12O6) and sodium chloride (NaCl) were purchased from Fine Chem. Potassium chloride was taken Thermo Fisher Scientific Pvt. Ltd. All the reagents were used without further process, and all the solutions were ready in distilled (DI) water.

The surface morphology and size analysis of CQDs was done with high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEOL JEM-2200FS, Japan). For TEM analysis, a 10–15 μL colloidal solution of CQDs was deposited and dried on a carbon-coated copper grid. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was used for functional group analysis of synthesized CQDs to analyze the functional group on the CQDs by using the universal ATR mode of Perkin Elmer Spectrum Two instruments. FTIR analysis was carried out by putting few drops of CQDs on a diamond crystal, and then record the spectra in the range of 400–4000 wavenumbers (cm−1). Surface charge of CQDs was calculated with zeta potential (ZEECOM, ZC-3000, Microtech. Japan). X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) was performed to find out the crystallinity of CQDs with Rigaku MiniFlex 600 X-Ray Diffractometer with CuKα radiation 41 (λ = 1.54 Å) operating at 40 kV and 15 mA. The XRD analysis of CQDs was carried the scan from 10°-60° at a scan rate of 5°/ minute with a step size of 0.02. The absorption and fluorescence spectra of the samples were recorded in T90 + UV/VIS spectrophotometer and Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (G9800A), respectively. For that, the colloidal solution of CQDs was taken in a 10 mm cuvette and scan the spectra. The fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs were recorded at excitation wavelength varies from 280 nm to 380 nm to determine the wavelength at which CQDs shows the maximum emission intensity. The excitation and emission slit width was kept at 5 nm. For pH value, measurement was carried out with μ pH system 362. The naked-eye visualization of CQDs was carried out under UV lamp irradiation in the UV fluorescence analysis cabinet. The change in decay lifetime of CQDs with and without MOXI was measured using time-correlating single photon counting (TCSPC, Fl-920, Edinburg Instruments).

CQDs were prepared using similar method used for the synthesis of polyvinyl-pyrrolidone (PVP) based CQDs (Ren et al., 2014). In this, one step hydrothermal method which is affordable, environmentally friendly, and nontoxic route is used to produce CQDs by using PVA as a source material for carbon. For synthesis 1.2 g PVA was taken in 40 mL of DI water and kept it under continuous stirring till transparent solution was obtained. After that, the mixture solution was transferred into a 100 mL teflon stainless steel autoclave and kept the reaction vessel at 240 °C for 9 hr., with a set rising time of 2.66 °C/ min. The reaction vessel then cooled to room temperature naturally. The transparent solution turns pale yellow at the end of the reaction, confirming CQDs' formation. The CQDs solution was stored at 4 °C for further use.

The fluorescence based determination of MOXI was executed through fluorescence spectrometer. DI water was used for preparation of 1 mM stock solution of MOXI. Detection of MOXI carried out in the presence of MOXI’s concentration varies from 0, 0.025, 0.125, 0.625, 1.25, 1.87, 2.5, 3.12, 3.75, 4.37, 5.0, 5.62, 6.25, 6.87, 7.5, 8.75, 10.0, 11.25, 12.5, 13.75, and, 15.0 µM in experimental solution in the presence of 100 µL of CQDs in a 2 mL volumetric tube and kept for 2 min incubation. Finally, fluorescence spectra were recorded at 300 nm excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interferents study for the selectivity of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality and selectivity of the sensor were tested with real food samples. The main motive of this work is to developed the sensor which could detect the reside of antibiotics mainly in animal derived food samples like milk and eggs. The selectivity test was performed in the presence of other antibiotics (AB) like, metabolites (MB), and metal ions (MI) which are presented in food samples. Here in this work wide range of interferents like antibiotics (AB) such as MOXI, LEVO, OFLO, NOR, GENTA, KMC, TETRA, AMPHI, AMOXI, THIO, FLOR metal ions (MI) like Na+, K+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Pb2+ and metabolites (MB) and proteins like ASPA A, GLU, AA, CA, UREA, CYS, URIC A, ARGI, METH, BSA 2 %, CASI, CHOL, and FA were taken which are presented in the real milk and egg samples and all other contaminated samples for the selectivity test. The interferents concentration was 5.0 μM, 50.0 μM, and 10.0 µM for AB, MB, and MI, respectively. Further, all the interferents spiked with standard MOXI concentration (5.0 µM) and then recorded fluorescence spectra of CQDs.

To calculate the efficacy and validation test of the sensor was done in milk and egg samples. Milk and eggs were bought from a local market Munirka, New Delhi, India. The real samples (milk, egg) were pretreated to removal the proteins and fat contains from milk and egg samples prior to their scanning test.

Milk sample preparation: Milk samples were treated by diluting it by 20-fold followed by 15 min centrifugation at 10,000 rpm to remove out the fat. The middle portion of centrifugation was taken and added 400 μL methanol in 1 mL sample in a new centrifuged tube to precipitate the protein and again centrifuged for another 15 min at conditions so that the proteins settled down in the form of pellet. The supernatant was taken out and tested for the presence of MOXI. When the sensor has no significance signal for MOXI in processed milk samples then after the milk were spiked with the known concentration of MOXI, and record the fluorescence spectra.
Egg sample Preparation: The egg sample were prepared by similar method as followed in our previous publications (Sajwan & Solanki, 2022).

The morphology and size of the synthesized CQDs were analyzed with TEM. Fig. 1 represents the particle size distribution and morphology of CQDs. TEM images show the quashi-spherical and mono-dispersed CQDs as presented in Fig. 1 (A). The lattice-resolved image of CQDs was obtained from HR-TEM and depicted inter-planar distance as 2.23 Å related to (002) plans (Sri et al., 2018) as shown in Fig. 1(B). Fig. 1 (C) presented the average particle size of CQDs was calculated as 5.9 ± 0.22 nm having wide range of size dispersion varies in the range of 3.0 to 8.4 nm.

Fig. 1(D) shows the functional group compositions of CQDs were characterized with the help of FTIR spectroscopy. In this work the CQDs were prepared using PVA as a source material for carbon. So that, the IR peak at 3526 cm−1 in FTIR spectra due to the presence of OH stretching in alcohol PVA. The anti-symmetric stretching of CH bond depicted by the peaks around 2952 cm−1 present in PVA (Ismail et al., 2023). The IR peak at 1698 cm−1 represented the existence of carbonyl group (CO) stretching vibration presented on the surface of CQDs. The presence of COO– and OH confirm through the IR peak at 1372 cm−1 and 1422 cm−1, respectively (Sajwan et al., 2021). The C–OH stretching vibration in the carboxylic group is confirmed by IR peak at 1048 cm−1. The average surface potential of CQDs was calculated as ζ = -6.7 mV using ZETA analyzer as shown in Fig. 1 (E). The negative zeta potential of CQDs confirms the presence of plenty of COO– and –OH groups on CQDs’s surface. The crystalline nature of CQDs was analyzed XRD characteristics as shown in Figure S1 in the supplementary data files. The amorphous peak around 22.08° was observed corresponding to graphitic (002) plane (John et al., 2022).

CQDs' absorbance and fluorescence spectra were investigated with UV–Visible and fluorescence spectrophotometers, as presented in Fig. 2 . The absorption spectra of CQDs as shown in curve (a) of Fig. 2 (A) shows a broad peak around 294 nm in the UV region corresponding to n → π * transition of the CO bond and its tail extended to the visible region (Sri et al., 2018). Curve (b) and (c) presents excitation and emission spectra of CQDs, respectively. The emission spectra of CQDs show bright emission at 372 nm at the excitation wavelength of 300 nm. The excitation wavelength of CQDs was selected on the bases of the maximum emission intensity emitted by CQDs at the excitation wavelength as shown in Fig. 2(B). From Fig. 2(B), it was clear that CQDs have an excitation-dependent emission that was shifted towards the wavelength with higher value with the increasing excitation wavelength varies from 280 nm to 380 nm. The excitation-dependent emission intensity results from quantum confinement and numerous emissive traps on the CQDs surface (Sarkar et al., 2018). The quantum yield (QY) of the synthesized CQDs was calculated according to the set procedure as described in details in the supplementary data files. The calculated value of QY was 2.12 % as shown in figure S2.

The proposed work is based on the fluorescence quenching of CQDs in addition to standard increasing MOXI concentration from 0.025 µM to 15.0 µM. Further to explaining the quenching mechanism by investigated the absorption, excitation and emission spectra of MOXI and CQDs, respectively. Fig. 3 (A) represented the absorption spectra of MOXI as curve (a) and excitation (b) and emission (c) spectra of CQDs. It is clearly depicted from the figure that the absorption spectra of MOXI is significantly overlapped with the excitation and emission spectra of CQDs. With the spectral overlapping the system fulfill the criteria of FRET and IFE acceptor–donor pairs based system (Yang et al., 2018). Here CQDs plays the role of donor and the MOXI is used as acceptor or quencher molecules. For, further confirmation of quenching mechanism, the decay time of CQDs in the absence and presence of MOXI were calculated using TCSPC techniques. TCSPC provides the valuable information about the excited state life times an electron spends between excited and emission state. Fig. 3(B) shows decay time of CQDs in presence and absence of MOXI as 3.5 nsec and 3.6 nsec, respectively. According to the criteria, if the life time or decay time of donor does not change in addition of acceptor then the quenching is associated with IFE and if the life time decreased in addition of acceptor then quenching belongs to FRET phenomenon. Here, life time of CQDs not changed in the presence of MOXI, which exclude the FRET mechanism and satisfied the condition of IFE quenching mechanism (Zu et al., 2017).

In the IFE, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs decreased with addition of increasing concentration of MOXI due to the absorption of donor emission when excited at 300 nm. When CQDs excited at 300 nm, it emits the blue emission peak centered at 372 nm. However, in the presence of MOXI the intensity of emission peak of CQDs at 372 nm goes diminish and a new emission peak at 508 nm was observed correspondence to MOXI (Sajwan et al., 2021) as shown in Figure S3. The schematic representation of quenching mechanism is shown in Fig. 3(C). The intensity at peak 372 nm decreased due to the IFE of increasing concentration of MOXI. However, the peak intensity at 508 nm increases along with MOXI’s concentration. For analyzing the quenching efficiency, the ratio between the intensity at peak 508 nm (F508) and at 372 nm (F372) was calculated as F508/F372. The higher value of F508/F372 indicate the higher quenching in presence of increasing concentration of MOXI.

The experimental parameters, such as pH value, incubation time, and amount of CQDs, were optimized to enhance the performance of the developed sensors with addition of MOXI. Figure S4 (A, B, and C) in supplementary data files represents the optimization result of the experimental parameters. When CQDs excited at 300 nm, they emit blue emission at 372 nm. In addition to MOXI, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs drastically decreased and emitted green emission at 508 nm. The change in the fluorescence signal was further calculated with the ratio between the emission intensity at F508 nm to F372 nm.

The pH study of CQDs was represented in Fig. S4 (A) in the 2.0 to 12.0. The CQDs' fluorescence intensity is high in at acidic medium, while it drastically decreases in the primary medium. The CQDs' strong response to pH is derived from the protonation and deprotonation of the amino and carboxyl groups on their surface. The result shows that the sensor fluorescence response signal decreased in acidic and basic conditions and gives best results at pH 6.7 (pH of DI). Besides the pH value, the incubation time is another essential factor that decides the sensor's response to the incubation time. Incubation time is when the sensor shows significant changes in its measuring signals within that period. For incubation time optimization the fluorescence intensity of CQDs in addition of MOXI was recorded with time intervals from 0 to 15 min, as shown in Fig. S4(B). From that, it is concluded that the interaction between CQDs and MOXI takes place very fast, and 2 min was sufficient time for the CQDs and MOXI interaction, and after that, the fluorescence signal not very much changed.

For the optimized performance, the concentration of CQDs plays a very curial role in the sensor's performance, so the optimized amount of CQDs plays significant role in sensing. In Fig. S4(C), it can be seen that with increasing amount of CQDs, the fluorescence signal is decreased therefore 100 μL of CQDs were chosen as an optimized value and used throughout the experiments. After optimization of all the experimental parameters and conditions, it was concluded that 100 μL of CQDs incubated for 2 min with MOXI at pH 6.7 gives the best output results.

After the evolution of all the sensing parameters which might influence the sensor's sensitivity and performance, the fluorescence spectra of CQDs were recorded along with the varying concentration of MOXI (0.025 μM – 15.0 μM). As denoted in Fig. 4 (A) the emission spectra of CQDs have centered maxima at the emission spectra 372 nm when excited at 300 nm wavelength. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs at peak 372 nm gradually decreased with varying concentration of MOXI as shwon in inset of Fig. 4(A) and a peak corresponding to the MOXI (Sajwan et al., 2021) at 508 nm was increased simultaneously along with increasing concentration of MOXI. The fluorescence quenching of CQDs in the presence of MOXI was analyzed with the ratio between the intensity peak observed at 508 nm (F508) and 372 nm (F372). The increasing value of the ratio (F508/F372) confirms the fluorescence quenching of CQDs in addition of MOXI due to the IFE phenomena takes place between CQDs and MOXI pairs as demonstrated in Fig. 4 (B).

The calculative ratio between the intensity of two peaks (F508/F372) was linearly proportional to the increasing concentration of MOXI varying from 0.025 μM – 15.0 μM with a respectable linear correlation (R2 = 0.9996). The sensor could detect the lowest (LOD) value of MOXI in the test samples was 6.34 nM which is lower than the set tolerance limit of antibiotics in food samples. The amount of MOXI quantified with the sensors was calculated in terms of LOQ as 20.92 nM. The LOD and LOQ was calculated with the formula LOD = 3σ/s, and LOQ = 3.3*LOD, where s is the slope of the linear calibration curve and σ represented standard deviation of the response curve (Sajwan & Solanki, 2023). The comparison between the present work and the reported work used for detecting MOXI is presented in Table 1 . The comparison data mentioned that the purposed sensor's linear detection range and LOD have improved compared to the earlier reported methods. So that the purposed sensor passes all the sensors conditions, like quick response selective to the analyte, is affordable and user friendly and could be used for further detection of MOXI in the food samples in a very simple manner.

The sensor's selectivity was evaluated in milk and egg samples. For that, significant source of interfering agents is tested, which is probably found in the real samples to avoid the hindrance during the real sample test. The potential interference like other AB, MB, and MI were taken for the test and analyzed their presence on sensor's performance. The concentrations of interments were chosen as 5.0 µM (AB), 50.0 µM (MB), and 10.0 µM (MI) along with 5.0 µM casein and 2 % BSA. Further the sensor performance was evaluated in the presence of all the interfering agents and records the fluorescence spectra of CQDs and calculated the ratio as shown in Fig. 4 (C) (purple color).

Further, the sensor's performance was studied with the spiked interference, which means all the interferents were spiked with the known standard concentration of MOXI (5.0 µM) and calculated the ratio as depicted in Fig. 4(C) (mustard color). From the interference graph, it is clearly visible that the sensor shows excellent recovery in the presence of MOXI.

After performing and analyzing all the parameters and the conditions the entire test, which might affect the performance of the sensor the real time application of the sensor was performed in the presence of real food samples like milk and egg. In first go the sensor was directly applied to processed milk and egg samples (as mentioned in section 3.3) and recorded the fluorescence spectra and negative results was obtained. Therefore, milk and egg samples were spiked with the known concentration of MOXI (2.5 µM, 5.0 µM and 7.5 µM) and then check the applicability of the proposed sensor in the presence of spiked samples.

The analytical outcome for real samples spiked with standard MOXI concentration is summarized in Figure S5. Figure S5 clearly depicted the change in intensity of CQDs in the presence of real samples before and after the addition of standard concentration of MOXI. Table 2 summarized all the results corresponds to milks and egg samples with recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD) values. The purposed sensor has RSD value lower than 6 % (2.06 % to 5.26 %) with good recovery rate varies from 95.14 % to 101.06 % justify the precision and consistency of the developed sensor for the detection of MOXI in practical applications.

In the present work, the CQDs were synthesized using one-port hydrothermal method by taking PVA first time as source precursor for carbon. The synthesized CQDs emitted bright blue emission centered at 372 nm with 2.12 % QY. The synthesized CQDs were quashi-spherical in nature and uniformly distributed having average particle around 5.9 ± 0.22 nm with 2.23 Å inter-planner distance. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs decreased in addition to increasing MOXI concentration. The quenching mechanism was investigated on the bases of spectral overlapping between absorption spectra of MOXI and the excitation /emission spectra of CQDs, and the life time decay of CQDs in absence and presence of MOXI was calculates as 3.5 nsec and 3.6 nsec, respectively. So that based on these results it was concluded that the emission intensity of CQDs quenched in presence of MOXI due to the IFE. The fluorescence of CQDs linearly quenched with increasing concentration of MOXI varying from 0.025 µM to 15.0 µM with LOD 6.34 nM and LOQ 20.92 nM. Further, the practicality of the developed sensor was calculated in spiked real milk and egg samples with good RSD (2.06 % to 5.26 %) and excellent recovery rate varies from 95.14 % to 101.06 %. Finally, all the experimental results confirm that the proposed sensor can be used in the food safety application.

Reena K. Sajwan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Jayendra Kumar Himanshu: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Pratima R. Solanki: Visualization, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work completes without technical and financial support provided by institute and Department of Science and Technology (DST). Authors are thankful to Advanced Instrumentation & Research Facility (AIRF) JNU, New Delhi, for providing a TEM and TRFS characterization facility. The author Reena K. Sajwan is also grateful to DST, India for providing financial support and motivation through Women Scientist- A (DST/WOS-A/ET-11/2020) fellowship. Solanki thanks to Indian Council of Medical Research, (PAC-SCNS-PS-ICMR-1293) New Delhi, India and Department Health Research, Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Government of India (Grant No: R.11013/46/2021-GIA/HR) for financial support.
